stimulus:
Percentage Point Changes in State Education Budget Relative to State GDP by Legislative Status

PeriodLegislative statusChange in total education budgetChange in K–12 budgetChange in higher-ed budget
1991–1994split−0.6−1.10.5
1995–1998unified1.81.40.4
1999–2002split−1.20.4−1.6
2003–2006unified0.70.50.2
2007–2010split2.11.60.5

Policy analyst Robin Tanaka has shown that split state legislatures—in which the two chambers are controlled by different parties—tend to accompany reductions in total state education budgets relative to state GDP, which Tanaka interprets to reflect tighter overall spending. Tanaka points to her calculations as evidence that a split legislature may be a “contributing but not decisive condition” for a reduction in total education budget to occur.
stem: Which choice best describes data from the table that support the underlined claim?

answerOptions.content: The periods of unified legislatures were associated with increases in K–12 budgets, whereas all periods of split legislatures were associated with reductions in higher-ed budgets.
All periods of split legislatures were associated with reductions in higher-ed budgets, although two of those periods were also associated with increases in K–12 budgets.
Most periods of split legislatures were associated with reductions in total education budget, whereas all periods of unified legislatures were associated with increases in total education budget.

The periods of unified legislatures were associated with increases in total education budget, whereas all periods of split legislatures were associated with reductions in either K–12 budget or higher-ed budget.

rationale: Choice C is the best answer because Tanaka’s claim is that split legislatures may be a contributing condition for total education-budget reductions. The data show two of the three split-legislature periods (1991–1994 and 1999–2002) had reductions in total budget while both unified periods saw increases. That matches the “contributing but not decisive” framing. Choice A oversimplifies higher-ed budget changes. Choice B focuses on K–12 not total. Choice D doesn’t describe the total-budget pattern.
